paragraph: Researchers at the Valdris Institute for Subatomic Studies announced on March 14, 2025, that the PRISM-9 detector array at the Halberg-Kran Particle Collider had recorded the highest-luminosity proton-proton collision run in the facility's history, achieving an integrated luminosity of 412 inverse femtobarns at a center-of-mass energy of 17.4 TeV. The record-breaking run, which spanned 22 consecutive days without beam interruption, produced approximately 1.7 billion collision events catalogued by the PRISM-9 tracking subsystem. Lead physicist Dr. Elenna Vorstmann stated that the data would be used to search for contact interactions at energy scales up to 48 TeV, extending the previous exclusion limit by nearly 11 TeV. The PRISM-9 detector, a 3,400-tonne hermetic spectrometer employing silicon pixel layers with 25-micron pitch and a 4-tesla superconducting solenoid, recorded a peak instantaneous luminosity of 9.1 times ten to the thirty-fifth per square centimeter per second during the final 48 hours of the run. Analysts from twelve collaborating universities are currently processing the raw event files using the PHASEMARK reconstruction pipeline, which applies a multivariate jet-tagging algorithm trained on 200 million simulated events to suppress background at a rejection rate exceeding five orders of magnitude.

paragraph: The NEUTRA-Flux Collaboration published results on May 2, 2025, reporting a 4.8-sigma observation of electron-neutrino appearance in a muon-neutrino beam at the Solgrenn Underground Facility, located 847 kilometers from the KRO-7 Synchrotron source. Using a 68-kiloton liquid-argon time-projection chamber designated LANTERN-III, the experiment detected 217 candidate electron-neutrino events against an expected background of 43.6 plus or minus 3.8 events, yielding a signal excess consistent with a CP-violating phase of delta-CP equal to negative 112 degrees at 68 percent confidence. The best-fit values of the atmospheric mixing parameters placed the squared mass splitting delta-m-32-squared at 2.51 times ten to the minus three square electron-volts, with mixing angle theta-23 at 49.1 degrees — marginally favoring the upper octant. Systematic uncertainties in the flux prediction were constrained to 2.4 percent using a near detector stationed 280 meters downstream of the KRO-7 extraction point. The collaboration, comprising 340 scientists from 31 institutions across 14 nations, noted that the measured appearance probability of 0.074 plus or minus 0.009 exceeded the no-CP-violation hypothesis by 4.1 sigma, representing the most significant direct evidence for leptonic CP violation reported to date.

paragraph: Scientists operating the VANTEX Muon Storage Ring at the Korveld Accelerator Complex released their fourth data-taking result on September 8, 2024, measuring the anomalous magnetic moment of the muon, known as g-minus-2, with a precision of 0.14 parts per million. The new value, a-mu equal to 116592089 times ten to the minus eleven with an uncertainty of 16 in the last two digits, was obtained from 6.2 times ten to the eleventh muon decay events collected during the VANTEX Run-4 campaign. When combined with the three preceding VANTEX datasets, the world-average experimental value of a-mu now deviates from the Korveld Standard Calculation Consortium's theoretical prediction by 5.3 sigma, corresponding to a probability of less than one in three million of arising by chance. The storage ring, which operates at a magic momentum of 3.094 GeV per c, maintained a field uniformity of 26 parts per billion across its 14.1-meter diameter, calibrated using a dedicated water-sample NMR probe array comprising 378 individual sensors. The collaboration emphasized that reducing beam-related systematic uncertainties, currently at 95 parts per billion, remains the primary target for the forthcoming Run-5 campaign beginning in early 2026.

paragraph: A team from the Brannfeld High-Energy Physics Group reported on January 19, 2025, that the SPECTRA-II detector at the Orlen Electron-Positron Facility had produced the most precise measurement of the Z boson decay width to invisible final states, yielding a value of 501.4 plus or minus 0.6 MeV. The measurement was derived from 14.3 million hadronic Z decays collected at center-of-mass energies between 88 and 94 GeV during a dedicated scan run conducted in autumn 2024. The invisible width is consistent with exactly three light neutrino generations, with an effective number of neutrino species of 2.994 plus or minus 0.006, a four-fold improvement in precision over previous measurements at the facility. Researchers applied an iterative radiative-correction procedure accounting for initial-state radiation, final-state photon emission, and electroweak vertex corrections at two-loop order, reducing theoretical uncertainties to below 0.2 MeV. The SPECTRA-II experiment employs a fine-grained electromagnetic calorimeter with 94,000 lead-tungstate crystals and a hadronic calorimeter read out by 48,000 scintillating-tile modules arranged in projective geometry around a 1.6-meter bore superconducting magnet producing a field of 5.2 tesla.

paragraph: The DARVEX-B Collaboration announced the observation of a new excited B-meson state on November 3, 2024, at a mass of 6341 plus or minus 4 plus or minus 8 MeV per c-squared, dubbed the B-star-star-u by the team, based on data from the DARVEX-B vertex spectrometer at the Heldmark Flavour Factory. The signal, produced in e-plus-e-minus collisions at a center-of-mass energy of 10.58 GeV, was observed with a local significance of 6.2 sigma in the B-plus-pi-minus invariant mass spectrum using a dataset of 980 inverse femtobarns. The natural width of the state was measured at 38 plus or minus 12 MeV, consistent with theoretical predictions for a D-wave orbital excitation of the bottom-up quark system. Differential decay angular distributions were analysed using a helicity formalism with seven independent amplitude parameters, and the dominant decay mode was identified as B-star-star-u decaying to B-plus-pi-minus with a relative branching fraction of 0.61 plus or minus 0.09. The discovery adds to a growing spectrum of bottom-quark mesons and provides stringent constraints on potential models of heavy-quark symmetry, with predictions from three competing frameworks tested against the measured mass, width, and spin-parity assignment of J-P equal to 2-plus.

paragraph: Physicists from the Tolvern Institute of Particle Science presented updated results from the SHADOW dark matter direct detection experiment on June 27, 2024, placing the world's most stringent limits on spin-independent WIMP-nucleon cross-sections for WIMP masses between 30 and 500 GeV per c-squared. Operated 2,180 meters underground at the Grenholt Deep Laboratory in a low-background environment with a muon flux of less than 0.3 per square meter per day, the SHADOW detector consists of a 14.7-tonne active mass of liquid xenon instrumented with 612 photomultiplier tubes in a dual-phase configuration. The dataset collected over 485.3 live-days yielded 3 candidate events in the signal region against a background expectation of 4.1 plus or minus 0.6 events, excluding a spin-independent cross-section of 3.8 times ten to the minus forty-eight square centimeters at 40 GeV per c-squared at 90 percent confidence. The radon emanation rate within the fiducial volume was measured at 0.13 millibecquerel per cubic meter using a dedicated alpha-spectroscopy system, and the electron recoil background in the 1 to 30 keV energy range was suppressed to 0.19 events per tonne per year using pulse-shape discrimination with a figure of merit of 0.91 at 50 percent nuclear recoil acceptance.

paragraph: The ATLAS-Prime Collaboration at the Halberg-Kran Particle Collider reported a search for vector-like top-quark partners on August 12, 2024, using 380 inverse femtobarns of proton-proton collision data at 17.4 TeV center-of-mass energy. No significant excess was observed above Standard Model predictions in the single-lepton plus multi-jet final state, leading to 95-percent confidence-level lower mass limits on vector-like T quarks of 1.97 TeV for the singlet representation and 2.21 TeV for the doublet representation assuming 100 percent branching ratio to t-Z. The analysis employed a boosted decision tree trained with 42 input variables, achieving signal-to-background separation with an area under the ROC curve of 0.89 in the high-mass signal region. Dominant systematic uncertainties arose from jet energy scale calibration at 2.3 percent and b-tagging efficiency at 1.8 percent. The collaboration also set limits on the vector-like T coupling parameter kappa-T, excluding values above 0.34 for masses below 1.5 TeV, which significantly constrains composite Higgs models in which the top quark partially emerges as a bound state of a new strongly interacting sector at scales of order 5 to 8 TeV.

paragraph: Researchers at the Pelrun Centre for Accelerator Science completed commissioning of the IRONGATE-III heavy-ion synchrotron on February 6, 2025, achieving gold-ion collisions at a record nucleon-nucleon center-of-mass energy of 5.52 TeV per nucleon pair. The first physics run produced quark-gluon plasma events with estimated initial temperatures exceeding 4.1 times ten to the twelfth kelvin, inferred from measurements of thermal photon yields and elliptic flow coefficients. The IRONGATE-III main ring, with a circumference of 7.8 kilometers and a maximum dipole field of 11.4 tesla achieved using Nb3Sn superconducting magnets cooled to 1.9 kelvin, accelerates fully stripped gold-197 nuclei to a kinetic energy of 2.76 TeV per nucleon before injection into the collision insertion region. Lead physicist Dr. Torben Halse announced that the first dataset of 12.4 million minimum-bias Au-Au events at full energy had been transferred to the PELRUN Computing Grid, where 18,000 processor cores are engaged in jet reconstruction, heavy-flavour tagging, and electromagnetic tomography of the dense partonic medium. The anisotropic flow coefficient v2 measured in mid-central collisions reached 0.081 plus or minus 0.004, consistent with nearly-perfect-fluid hydrodynamic models with a shear viscosity to entropy density ratio of 1.2 times the Kovtun-Son-Starinets bound.

paragraph: The HELIX-B experiment at the Dornmark Flavour Collider released on October 14, 2024, a measurement of the branching fraction of the rare decay B-zero to K-zero-short plus mu-plus-mu-minus, obtaining a value of 3.81 plus or minus 0.22 plus or minus 0.17 times ten to the minus seven — the most precise measurement of this mode. The dataset of 1.4 billion B-meson pairs enabled rejection of background from B-zero to psi-2S-K-zero-short to a level below 0.4 percent of the signal yield using a two-dimensional sideband subtraction technique. The differential branching fraction as a function of the dimuon invariant mass squared q-squared was measured in eight q-squared bins between 1.1 and 19 GeV-squared, revealing a deficit relative to the theoretical form-factor prediction of Lattice QCD group FORMA-6 in the bin 6 to 8 GeV-squared at the level of 2.4 sigma. Angular observables P5-prime and S5 were extracted using a six-dimensional unbinned maximum-likelihood fit, yielding P5-prime of minus 0.31 plus or minus 0.11 in the 4 to 6 GeV-squared bin, consistent with the pattern of anomalies reported previously by several competing experiments and suggestive of new physics contributions to the Wilson coefficient C9.

paragraph: The GRAVITON-Search Consortium released exclusion limits on graviton resonances at the Orlen Linear Collider Complex on March 28, 2025, using a dataset of 295 inverse femtobarns of electron-positron collisions at 3.0 TeV center-of-mass energy. No evidence for spin-2 graviton production was found in the di-photon, di-lepton, or di-jet channels, and 95-percent CL upper limits on the production cross-section times branching ratio ranged from 0.11 femtobarns for a 1.5 TeV graviton to 0.024 femtobarns for a 2.8 TeV graviton in the Randall-Sundrum analogue model framework constructed by theorists at the Forsberg Institute. The di-photon search achieved a diphoton mass resolution of 0.9 percent at 2.0 TeV using a crystal electromagnetic calorimeter with an energy resolution term of 2.4 percent over root-E and a stochastic term of 0.11 percent. Spin analysis of candidate di-photon events above 1.8 TeV was performed using the Collins-Soper angular distribution, and the data are consistent with the spin-1 exclusion required by Yang's theorem. Combined with earlier runs at 1.4 and 2.0 TeV at the same facility, the exclusion on the effective graviton coupling k over M-Pl exceeds 0.12 for graviton masses below 2.6 TeV.

paragraph: Physicists from the Novard Particle Physics Laboratory announced on July 7, 2024, a 5.1-sigma detection of the doubly charged Higgs boson H-plus-plus in the four-lepton final state at the Rolfsen Circular Collider, using 210 inverse femtobarns at 14.8 TeV. The signal was reconstructed at a mass of 448 plus or minus 6 MeV per c-squared above the Drell-Yan expectation at that lepton invariant mass, with a production cross-section of 1.43 plus or minus 0.22 femtobarns consistent with predictions from the Type-II seesaw extension of the Standard Model. The four-lepton invariant mass resolution at the signal mass was 2.8 GeV, dominated by final-state radiation and electron bremsstrahlung in the inner tracker material. The H-plus-plus decay was observed preferentially to tau-plus-tau-plus with a partial width ratio to e-plus-mu-plus of 3.1 plus or minus 0.6, providing evidence for non-universal Yukawa couplings in the triplet Higgs sector. The analysis used 120 million simulated pp events generated with the PARTON-9 Monte Carlo program at NLO-QCD accuracy and matched to a PS shower based on the Novard dipole shower scheme, achieving a data-to-simulation agreement within 5 percent in all control regions.

paragraph: A paper submitted to Physical Review Letters on April 18, 2025, by the CHARMED-W Collaboration described the first observation of the doubly charmed baryon Xi-cc-plus-plus decay to Xi-c-plus-pi-plus, with a signal yield of 845 plus or minus 67 events reconstructed in 640 inverse femtobarns of data from the Heldmark Flavour Factory. The decay was isolated using a gradient-boosted classifier trained on 14 simulated variables including vertex flight distance, impact parameter chi-squared, and particle identification likelihoods from the ring-imaging Cherenkov detector. The Xi-cc-plus-plus mass was measured at 3621.2 plus or minus 0.7 plus or minus 1.1 MeV per c-squared, and its lifetime was determined to be 256 plus or minus 24 plus or minus 14 femtoseconds from a vertex fit using 389 four-body decays in a separate control sample. The product of the production cross-section and relative branching fraction was found to be 8.4 plus or minus 0.9 times ten to the minus four relative to the Lambda-c-plus, exceeding most perturbative QCD predictions and motivating revisiting of fragmentation models for doubly heavy baryons. The result opens a new window for testing heavy quark expansion predictions for baryons containing two charm quarks.

paragraph: The POLARON-X Experiment at the Valdris Underground Accelerator published constraints on sterile neutrino mixing on December 5, 2024, using an electron-neutrino disappearance analysis with a 22-tonne reactor antineutrino detector placed at a baseline of 18 meters from the Valdris Research Reactor No. 3, which operates at a thermal power of 24 megawatts. A total of 2.84 million reactor-antineutrino interactions were detected over 613 reactor-on days, yielding an observed-to-expected ratio of 0.937 plus or minus 0.011 after applying an energy-dependent oscillation correction. The shape-plus-rate analysis excluded the Reactor Antineutrino Anomaly parameter space in the region delta-m-squared above 0.4 eV-squared at 95 percent CL, covering the sterile mixing angle sin-squared 2 theta-ee between 0.04 and 0.90. The detector deployed a near-total containment design using 14,400 gadolinium-doped liquid scintillator cells with photon detection efficiency of 62 percent, yielding a hydrogen-capture delayed-coincidence tagging efficiency of 78.4 plus or minus 0.9 percent. Backgrounds from accidental coincidences were suppressed to below 0.3 percent of the signal using a 200-microsecond muon veto applied after cosmic-ray tag signals from the 4-pi water-Cherenkov muon shield surrounding the detector.

paragraph: Scientists at the Grenfeld Collider Laboratory at the Solhaven Technical University announced on February 22, 2025, the measurement of the top-quark mass using a novel lepton-plus-MET template method applied to 170 inverse femtobarns of proton-proton data at 17.4 TeV from the PRISM-9 detector. The measured top mass in the MS-bar renormalization scheme at a renormalization scale of 170 GeV was found to be 162.7 plus or minus 0.4 (stat.) plus or minus 0.9 (syst.) plus or minus 0.6 (theory) GeV per c-squared. This represents a 28 percent reduction in total uncertainty relative to the previous best measurement and is the first top-mass determination to directly access a short-distance mass scheme, circumventing the theoretical ambiguity of the Monte Carlo mass parameter. The method exploits the relationship between the lepton energy distribution and the top-quark mass at NLO accuracy matched to parton showers, with a linear sensitivity of 2.4 GeV in lepton energy per GeV change in top-quark mass. Theoretical uncertainties were evaluated using 15 independent PDF variations from the PDFMASTER-23 ensemble and five renormalization and factorization scale choices, with all contributions included in a full profiling procedure implemented in the PRISM-9 analysis framework NEXUSFIT version 4.2.

paragraph: The EPSILON-Tau Lepton Universality Experiment at the Pelrun Collider Center reported on May 19, 2025, a measurement of the ratio of branching fractions R-K equal to B-plus to K-plus-tau-plus-tau-minus over B-plus to K-plus-mu-plus-mu-minus in the q-squared range 15 to 22 GeV-squared, yielding R-K equal to 0.71 plus or minus 0.11 plus or minus 0.04 — a 2.8-sigma deviation from the Standard Model expectation of unity. The analysis was based on 410 inverse femtobarns of data from the Pelrun Lepton Collider, with tau leptons reconstructed in the three-prong hadronic decay channel and separated from backgrounds using a multivariate isolation discriminant with a signal efficiency of 38 percent and a QCD multijet rejection factor of 2400. Kinematic endpoint variables were used to suppress semileptonic B-meson backgrounds, achieving a combinatorial background purity exceeding 97 percent in the signal window. Systematic uncertainties related to the tau-identification efficiency were constrained to 4.3 percent using a J/psi to tau-tau control sample. The result, when interpreted in the context of an effective field theory framework including vector and axial-vector leptoquark mediators, favors a coupling structure with a new-physics scale of approximately 31 TeV dominating the third-generation lepton sector.

paragraph: The NEXUS-Higgs Collaboration at the Halberg-Kran Particle Collider presented on August 5, 2024, the most stringent constraints on Higgs boson self-coupling through di-Higgs production, using 380 inverse femtobarns at 17.4 TeV. In the bb-gamma-gamma final state, which benefits from a clean di-photon invariant mass peak, the observed 95-percent CL upper limit on di-Higgs production was found to be 2.4 times the Standard Model prediction, while the expected limit in the absence of signal was 1.9 times the Standard Model. The constraint on the trilinear Higgs self-coupling modifier kappa-lambda was set to the interval from minus 1.7 to plus 6.1 at 95 percent CL, tightening the previous measurement by 22 percent due in part to a new neural-network-based m-HH reconstruction technique that improved signal-to-background ratio in the high-mass regime by a factor of 1.8. The di-photon mass window was defined as 122 to 130 GeV to maximize signal retention while controlling QCD diphoton background using a polynomial order-5 fit anchored in sidebands. A dedicated scan for anomalous Higgs-top coupling modifications was also performed, excluding contact interaction terms above a scale of 2.3 TeV at 68 percent CL.

paragraph: The CORONA-nu Atmospheric Neutrino Collaboration published improved neutrino-nucleon cross-section measurements on October 2, 2024, using a 50-kiloton magnetised iron tracking calorimeter at the Thornhaven Deep Laboratory, operating at a depth of 3800 meters water-equivalent. Using a sample of 94,000 contained atmospheric neutrino interactions collected over 13.6 years of operation, the collaboration measured the charged-current inclusive cross-section for muon neutrinos on iron nuclei at mean energies of 2.3, 7.1, and 22.4 GeV, finding values of 5.8 plus or minus 0.3, 17.1 plus or minus 0.8, and 52.4 plus or minus 3.1 times ten to the minus thirty-nine square centimeters per nucleon respectively. These measurements are consistent with the LHAPDF-6 nuclear parton distribution function set within 1.3 sigma at all three energy points. The magnetised geometry of the detector, with a central field of 1.3 tesla, allowed separate measurements for neutrino and antineutrino interactions with a charge-identification purity of 97.2 percent for muon momenta above 2 GeV per c. The result provides important input to atmospheric oscillation analyses by constraining the neutrino flux model uncertainty from 6.5 to 3.1 percent in the 5 to 50 GeV range.

paragraph: Scientists from the Falkenberg Institute for Theoretical and Experimental Physics reported on January 30, 2025, that the QUARK-7 detector at the Rolfsen Circular Collider had observed the decay of a Bc-plus meson to three muons and a neutrino — specifically Bc-plus to J/psi-mu-plus-nu-mu — with 1,240 signal events reconstructed in 310 inverse femtobarns. The measured product branching fraction relative to Bc-plus to J/psi-pi-plus was 19.4 plus or minus 1.9 plus or minus 2.3, consistent with predictions from lattice QCD carried out by the QUARKLAT Consortium placing the central value at 18.1 plus or minus 2.6. Extraction of the CKM matrix element V-cb using the measured partial rate and the lattice form factors yielded a value of V-cb equal to 41.2 plus or minus 2.1 times ten to the minus three, with the dominant uncertainty arising from form-factor normalization at the 3.1 percent level. Muon identification relied on matching track segments from the central silicon tracker to drift-tube hits in the muon system with a chi-squared per degree of freedom below 2.5, achieving a misidentification rate of 0.4 percent for pions above 8 GeV. The result represents the first observation of this semileptonic mode and validates the approach of using Bc decays as precision electroweak probes.

paragraph: The FLUX-Sentinel neutrino telescope at the Solvang Polar Station, a cubic-kilometer scale water-based Cherenkov detector deployed at a depth of 1,900 meters in the polar ice sheet, released its latest diffuse astrophysical neutrino spectrum analysis on April 9, 2025. Using 11 years of muon-neutrino track data corresponding to an effective exposure of 7.4 times ten to the third square-kilometer years, the collaboration found best-fit spectral parameters for an unbroken power-law flux of E-squared times phi equal to 2.37 plus or minus 0.18 times ten to the minus eight GeV per square centimeter per second per steradian at 100 TeV, with a spectral index of minus 2.41 plus or minus 0.07. A spectral feature at 1.3 PeV was identified at 2.4 sigma local significance, consistent with a contribution from a source population with a harder spectrum of index minus 2.1. The galactic plane emission component, separated by morphological templates, contributed approximately 14 plus or minus 4 percent of the total flux above 10 TeV. The analysis excluded a purely atmospheric neutrino origin at greater than 8 sigma based on the energy-dependent zenith angle distribution, and three candidate sources were found in spatial correlation with catalogued blazars within the FLUXCAT-22 gamma-ray catalogue.

paragraph: The KALEIDOSCOPE-B Experiment at the Dornmark Flavour Collider published the most precise measurement of the CKM angle phi-3 (also denoted gamma) on July 21, 2024, combining twelve B-decay modes in a frequentist combination framework. The resulting measurement of phi-3 equals 71.4 plus or minus 3.2 degrees was obtained from a dataset of 1.2 inverse attobarns collected over eight years of operation, with the statistical precision limited primarily by the sample of 2,870 B-minus to D-K-minus decays in the CP-eigenstate mode. The dominant systematic uncertainty of 1.1 degrees arose from model-dependence in the Dalitz-plot description of the three-body D-meson decay, constrained with a dedicated quantum-correlated D-meson dataset of 3.4 million events collected at the charm threshold at 3.77 GeV. An independent model-independent analysis using bin-by-bin coherence factors from the CHARMED Quantum Correlation experiment yielded phi-3 equal to 72.1 plus or minus 3.4 degrees, confirming the Dalitz-model result within uncertainties. Combined with unitarity triangle fits using external inputs for V-ub, V-cb, B-mixing parameters, and epsilon-K, the new gamma measurement yields indirect constraints fully consistent with direct measurements, with no indication of new physics contributions to B-meson mixing at the 1.3 percent level.

paragraph: Researchers at the Pelrun Accelerator Institute unveiled on March 3, 2025, the design specifications for the HERCULES-IV proton synchrotron, a proposed facility operating at a center-of-mass energy of 100 TeV with a circumference of 91 kilometers to be sited in the Pelrun Upland region northeast of Solhaven. The design relies on 16-tesla dipole magnets using high-temperature superconducting REBCO tapes cooled to 20 kelvin, reducing cryogenic power consumption by a factor of 3.2 compared to conventional liquid-helium-cooled designs. The target peak luminosity is 2.5 times ten to the thirty-fifth per square centimeter per second, achievable with 50,000 proton bunches per ring separated by 25 nanoseconds, each containing 2.3 times ten to the eleventh protons. Studies by the Grenfeld Physics Working Group project that HERCULES-IV would produce 3.7 million Higgs bosons per year in optimum running conditions, enabling measurement of the Higgs self-coupling to 4 percent precision and direct searches for new particles up to masses of 44 TeV in pair production. The total estimated construction cost, presented in the Pelrun Accelerator Design Report version 3.1, is 26.4 billion international research units, with a construction timeline of 17 years from groundbreaking to first physics delivery.

paragraph: The SIGMAJET Collaboration at the KRO-7 Synchrotron reported on November 17, 2024, a first measurement of the triple-differential dijet cross-section as a function of dijet invariant mass, rapidity separation, and azimuthal angle decorrelation, using 280 inverse femtobarns of proton-proton data at 14.0 TeV center-of-mass energy. The measurement spans dijet masses from 2.0 to 14.0 TeV and rapidity separations from zero to 3.0 units, testing perturbative QCD at NNLO accuracy for the first time across the full kinematic range. The largest deviation from theoretical prediction was found at rapidity separations above 2.5 units in the 2.0 to 3.0 TeV mass bin, where data exceeded the NNLO prediction by 11 plus or minus 4 percent — consistent with BFKL resummation effects at high t-channel momentum transfer. Jet energies were calibrated in situ using Z-plus-jet events, achieving a jet energy scale uncertainty below 1.0 percent for jets with transverse momentum above 50 GeV. The triple-differential measurement used 2.4 million selected dijet events with leading jet transverse momentum above 600 GeV, with multijet background estimated by a matrix element method validated against dedicated three-jet control samples collected simultaneously during the physics run.

paragraph: The COLDVAULT-II axion dark matter search experiment at the Brannfeld Underground Laboratory published on September 25, 2024, exclusion limits on the axion-photon coupling constant g-a-gamma over the mass range from 1.7 to 6.4 micro-electron-volts, operating a microwave cavity resonator cooled to 15 millikelvin by a dilution refrigerator with a base cooling power of 800 microwatts. The cavity, fabricated from oxygen-free high-conductivity copper with an internal volume of 14.2 liters, operated in a 9.4-tesla superconducting solenoid magnetic field, achieving a quality factor Q of 1.05 times ten to the sixth during the analysis run. Signal power at the cavity output was amplified by a Josephson parametric amplifier with a system noise temperature of 0.41 kelvin, approaching the standard quantum limit within a factor of 3.4. Scanning at a rate of 320 Hz per second in frequency, the experiment set limits on g-a-gamma below the benchmark KSVZ axion model line at 1.9 times ten to the minus fifteen inverse GeV across a mass range of 4.7 micro-electron-volts, excluding the standard cosmological dark matter axion hypothesis over 68 percent of the targeted mass range at 95 percent CL. The next phase, COLDVAULT-III, plans to deploy a 62-liter cavity array to extend coverage to 9 micro-electron-volts.

paragraph: A joint announcement from the TESSERA Neutrino Observatory and the QUARK-7 experiment on December 12, 2024, reported the first measurement of tau-neutrino appearance in a high-energy muon-neutrino beam, yielding 74 tau-neutrino candidate events in a 12-tonne OPERA-analogue nuclear-emulsion detector station at the Heldmark underground site, 735 kilometers from the KRO-7 neutrino source. The measured signal exceeds the no-oscillation expectation by 7.4 sigma, and the tau-appearance probability integrated over the energy spectrum is 0.052 plus or minus 0.007, consistent with maximal theta-23 mixing and delta-m-32-squared of 2.51 times ten to the minus three square electron-volts. A detailed analysis of the tau-decay topologies distinguished one-prong hadronic, three-prong hadronic, and leptonic channels, with 24, 31, and 19 events respectively — in good agreement with branching-fraction-weighted predictions. The emulsion detector was refreshed five times during the run, and individual tau-decay vertices were located using automated scanning stations at 4.7 centimeters per second per square centimeter, achieving a vertex resolution of 0.4 microns transverse and 1.6 microns longitudinal. The result constitutes the direct proof of tau-neutrino appearance at distances of order one thousand kilometers.

paragraph: Physicists from the Yelverton Institute at the Solhaven University published on April 4, 2025, a study of electroweak penguin decays of B-zero mesons at the HELIX-B experiment at the Dornmark Flavour Collider, measuring the time-dependent CP asymmetry in B-zero to phi-K-zero-short with a signal sample of 8,340 events from 1.2 inverse attobarns of e-plus-e-minus data at the Upsilon-4S resonance. The measured CP-violation parameters were S equal to minus 0.61 plus or minus 0.06 plus or minus 0.02 and C equal to 0.02 plus or minus 0.05, where S represents the mixing-induced asymmetry and C the direct CP asymmetry. The value of S is consistent with the Standard Model prediction of minus sine of two-beta equal to minus 0.69 at the level of 1.3 sigma, which is the least significant tension observed in this mode. Penguin pollution corrections from the colour-suppressed charm-loop amplitude were estimated using Soft Collinear Effective Theory at next-to-leading logarithmic order, shifting the theoretical prediction for S by 0.04 and reducing the hadronic uncertainty to 0.03. The combined world average of CP asymmetries in b-to-s-quark transitions including this measurement still shows a pattern suggesting modest suppression of the Standard Model amplitude at the level of 1.8 sigma.

paragraph: The BEAMCORE-IV fixed-target experiment at the Kolvern Proton Synchrotron Complex published on February 14, 2025, a measurement of the proton electric and magnetic Sachs form factors in the spacelike region 0.5 to 14.2 GeV-squared using deep elastic scattering of 800-GeV protons on a 30-centimeter liquid hydrogen target. The ratio G-E over G-M was determined using the Rosenbluth separation technique at 38 kinematic settings, with a final dataset of 2.2 million elastic events selected from a total of 140 inverse femtobarns. The measured ratio falls monotonically from 1.02 at Q-squared equal to 0.5 GeV-squared to 0.28 at Q-squared equal to 14.2 GeV-squared, confirming the pattern seen in earlier transverse polarisation transfer measurements and inconsistent with dipole form-factor scaling above 8 GeV-squared at the 3.2-sigma level. Coulomb distortion corrections were applied using a partial-wave expansion calculated to order Z-alpha-squared, and higher-twist contributions were estimated from a twist-four QCD fit to structure function data from the BEAMCORE-III run, contributing at most 1.7 percent of the measured cross-section at the highest Q-squared point. The new data provide improved constraints on two-photon-exchange amplitudes using dispersion relation analyses, with the real part of the box contribution constrained to 1.2 percent at Q-squared equal to 10 GeV-squared.

paragraph: The RESONANCE-B Collaboration at the Dornmark Flavour Collider released on August 30, 2024, an updated measurement of the semileptonic ratio R-D-star equal to the branching fraction of B-bar to D-star-plus-tau-minus-nu-tau divided by B-bar to D-star-plus-l-minus-nu-l for light leptons l, finding R-D-star equal to 0.293 plus or minus 0.011 plus or minus 0.006 from a sample of 64,000 signal candidates reconstructed using a full hadronic tag method in 980 inverse femtobarns of data. This measurement deviates from the Standard Model prediction of 0.258 plus or minus 0.005 computed by the HFLAV-Novard theory group at the level of 2.8 sigma when considered in isolation. The tau lepton was reconstructed exclusively in the one-prong electronic, one-prong muonic, and one-prong hadronic modes, with yields extracted using a two-dimensional fit to the visible energy and missing mass squared of the entire hadronic event. The correlation between the R-D-star and the simultaneously measured R-D equal to 0.347 plus or minus 0.032 is minus 0.41, and the combined significance of both deviations from the Standard Model is 4.1 sigma, consistent with new physics involving enhanced coupling to third-generation leptons. Template shapes for the D-star-tau-nu signal were derived from a heavy-quark effective theory form-factor parameterisation updated to include sub-leading Isgur-Wise functions at one over m-c-squared.

paragraph: Researchers from the SKYWIRE Cosmic-Ray Array Collaboration, a 3,000-square-kilometer ground-based extensive air shower detector network at the Altiplano of Volkerstein, announced on June 3, 2025, the detection of 14 ultra-high-energy cosmic rays with energies above 5 times ten to the nineteenth eV over seven years of operation, including one event with a reconstructed energy of 2.4 times ten to the twentieth eV — the highest cosmic-ray energy recorded since the Volkerstein Array began operations in 2018. The event was reconstructed using 312 triggered water-Cherenkov stations in a footprint of 180 square kilometers, with a shower maximum Xmax of 788 plus or minus 22 grams per square centimeter, favoring a proton or helium primary composition at the 68 percent CL level. Angular reconstruction precision for the highest-energy event was 0.3 degrees, and back-tracking through the Galactic and extragalactic magnetic fields using a structured field model identified NGC 5432 analogue galaxy VK-04411 as a plausible source within a 3-degree cone. The energy spectrum above the GZK suppression region exhibits a spectral slope change from minus 2.6 to minus 4.3 at a break energy of 4.1 times ten to the nineteenth eV, consistent with proton photopion production on the cosmic microwave background for a source distance below 40 Megaparsecs.

paragraph: The PHANTASM long-baseline accelerator neutrino experiment submitted on March 18, 2025, an analysis of the delta-CP parameter using a joint fit of muon-neutrino disappearance and electron-neutrino appearance data from both neutrino and antineutrino running modes at the Grenholt Neutrino Beam Facility. The beam, produced by 120-GeV protons from the Grenholt Main Injector striking a graphite target with 900 kilowatts of beam power, delivers a muon-neutrino flux peaked at 2.6 GeV to the 620-tonne near detector and the 31.5-kiloton far detector at a distance of 1,290 kilometers. Combining 21.3 times ten to the twentieth protons on target in neutrino mode and 16.7 times ten to the twentieth in antineutrino mode, the analysis observes 367 electron-neutrino appearance events against a background of 81.4, and the best-fit value of delta-CP is minus 143 degrees, excluding the CP-conserving values of zero and 180 degrees at 2.4 sigma. The normal mass ordering is preferred over the inverted ordering with a chi-squared difference of 5.6. The analysis uses the GENIE-4 neutrino interaction model with a custom nuclear effects package tuned to carbon data from the PHANTASM near detector, reducing flux-times-cross-section uncertainty to 4.8 percent at the far detector.

paragraph: The LIGHTBRIDGE-Tau Experiment at the Grenfeld Collider Laboratory announced on October 28, 2024, a search for lepton-flavour-violating tau decays in 840 inverse femtobarns of e-plus-e-minus data at the Upsilon-4S resonance, setting 90-percent CL upper limits on 34 individual tau decay modes. The most stringent limits were placed on tau-minus to e-minus-gamma at 2.4 times ten to the minus nine, tau-minus to mu-minus-gamma at 3.1 times ten to the minus nine, and tau-minus to three muons at 1.7 times ten to the minus nine, all representing improvements of a factor of 2 to 5 over prior measurements. The analysis used a novel signal-region optimization based on expected-limit minimization as a function of signal-to-background cut values, evaluated in a blinded fashion on a simulated dataset of 80 million signal and 5 billion background events. Photon detection efficiency in the tau-to-lepton-gamma modes was calibrated using radiative Bhabha events with an absolute systematic uncertainty of 1.3 percent. The results constrain effective operators in a dimension-6 effective field theory parameterisation, excluding the scale of lepton-flavour-violating dipole operators involving a photon above 270 TeV for the mu-gamma mode and above 310 TeV for the e-gamma mode at 90 percent confidence level.

paragraph: The BOSON-SCAN Precision Measurement Program at the Orlen Electron-Positron Facility delivered on May 8, 2025, the first simultaneous determination of the W boson mass and the electroweak mixing angle sin-squared-theta-W from a single dataset of 84 million W-boson decays to electron-neutrino pairs collected at center-of-mass energies from 160 to 210 GeV. The W mass was measured to be 80,421.4 plus or minus 8.6 MeV per c-squared, consistent with the Standard Model prediction of 80,353 plus or minus 7 MeV but 1.4 sigma higher. The effective electroweak mixing angle in the on-shell scheme was determined to be 0.23158 plus or minus 0.00021, the most precise single-experiment determination to date. The measurements were extracted using a template-fitting approach with W-boson transverse mass and lepton transverse momentum as primary observables, with photon radiation corrections applied at next-to-next-to-leading order using the PHOTRADCOR-5 library. Lepton energy calibration was performed in situ using Z boson decays, achieving an energy scale uncertainty of 1.9 MeV for electrons and a momentum scale uncertainty of 2.3 MeV per c for muons. The combination of the W mass and sin-squared-theta-W provides a global electroweak fit sensitivity competitive with the world average from the previous generation of experiments.

paragraph: Scientists from the Heldmark Institute for Heavy Ion Physics reported on July 14, 2024, measurements of charm and bottom quark energy loss in the quark-gluon plasma created in gold-gold collisions at the IRONGATE-III heavy-ion collider. Using the TORCHBEAM detector, which incorporates a silicon vertex detector with a track impact parameter resolution of 17 microns, the collaboration measured the nuclear modification factor R-AA of D-zero mesons in the 0 to 10 percent centrality class at mid-rapidity, finding suppression of a factor 4.6 plus or minus 0.4 relative to the proton-proton reference at transverse momenta between 8 and 20 GeV per c. The R-AA of B-plus mesons in the same centrality class was 2.9 plus or minus 0.3 at the same transverse momentum range, demonstrating the mass-dependent flavor hierarchy of radiative energy loss in the deconfined medium predicted by dead-cone effect calculations. The ratio R-AA(D)/R-AA(B) of 1.58 plus or minus 0.18 exceeds the DGLV transport coefficient model prediction of 1.28 by 1.7 sigma, suggesting additional collisional energy loss contributions not included in the baseline calculation. The measurement was enabled by a dedicated displaced-vertex trigger operating at an event rate of 12 kHz with a heavy-flavour enrichment factor of 8 compared to minimum-bias collisions.

paragraph: The HIGGS-PORTAL Precision Program at the Halberg-Kran Particle Collider published on September 16, 2024, a combined measurement of Higgs boson couplings using 17 production and decay channels in 380 inverse femtobarns at 17.4 TeV. In the kappa-framework analysis, all coupling modifiers were found consistent with Standard Model predictions within 1.4 sigma, with the most precise individual determinations being kappa-Z equal to 1.023 plus or minus 0.022, kappa-W equal to 1.011 plus or minus 0.025, kappa-b equal to 0.98 plus or minus 0.04, and kappa-tau equal to 1.04 plus or minus 0.04. The constraint on BSM Higgs decay branching fractions not captured by the measured modes yielded an upper limit of 6.4 percent at 95 percent CL, a 30 percent improvement over previous results. The Higgs boson signal strength in the mu-plus-mu-minus decay channel was measured at 1.11 plus or minus 0.39, providing 3.2-sigma evidence for the Higgs coupling to second-generation fermions. Production mode signal strengths in the gluon-gluon fusion, vector boson fusion, associated production, and ttH associated production channels were measured with relative uncertainties of 4.2, 5.8, 7.1, and 9.3 percent respectively. The global compatibility of the coupling measurements with the Standard Model hypothesis was quantified by a p-value of 0.42.

paragraph: The PRISMOID Low-Energy Muon Capture Experiment at the Valdris Meson Factory published on December 22, 2024, a measurement of the induced pseudoscalar coupling g-P-star using muon capture on hydrogen, finding g-P-star equal to 8.06 plus or minus 0.55 — a result that represents the first test of chiral perturbation theory predictions for this quantity at the 6.8 percent level. The experiment used a high-pressure hydrogen time-projection chamber operating at 10 atmospheres with an active volume of 280 liters, detecting the 5.2-MeV muon-capture neutron via a surrounding array of 60 liquid-scintillator detectors with a total efficiency of 34.7 percent. The muon stopping rate in the active volume was 7,400 per second during the 14-month production run, yielding 1.92 times ten to the ninth recorded muon stops and 4,420 identified capture events above a neutron energy threshold of 3 MeV. The dominant systematic uncertainty of 3.4 percent arose from neutron detection efficiency modelling, constrained using a calibration source of californium-252 placed at five positions inside the TPC. The ChPT prediction at next-to-next-to-leading order gives g-P-star equal to 8.26 plus or minus 0.23, consistent with the measurement and representing a 0.3-sigma agreement that validates the chiral Lagrangian approach to nucleon matrix elements.

paragraph: Physicists from the Rondell Accelerator Science Centre and the Pelrun Institute jointly reported on February 28, 2025, the commissioning results of the VERTIGO muon collider demonstrator ring, achieving the first muon-antimuon acceleration to 45 GeV in a 400-meter circumference ring at the Rondell Research Park. Muons were produced from pion decays following 6-GeV proton interactions on a beryllium target, yielding an initial muon intensity of 3.8 times ten to the eighth per pulse at a repetition rate of 15 Hz. Phase-space cooling of the muon beam was demonstrated using a liquid-hydrogen ionisation absorber 40 centimeters long, reducing the transverse normalized emittance from 11.4 to 4.3 millimeter-radians — a 62 percent reduction consistent with design expectations. Acceleration in the demonstration ring used 24 superconducting niobium-titanium cavities operating at 1.3 GHz with an average accelerating gradient of 28 MeV per meter. The beam lifetime at 45 GeV, dominated by muon decay with a Lorentz-dilated mean life of 2.14 milliseconds, was demonstrated to be sufficient for six complete turns before intensity falls below 10 percent. The VERTIGO results strengthen the physics case for a future 10-TeV center-of-mass muon collider capable of producing 1.4 million Higgs bosons per year in the s-channel.

paragraph: The PHASEMARK-LQ Collaboration at the Rolfsen Circular Collider published on November 4, 2024, a search for pair production of scalar leptoquarks decaying to a top quark and a tau lepton in 310 inverse femtobarns of data at 14.8 TeV. No excess was observed above the Standard Model expectation of 8.1 plus or minus 1.4 background events in the signal region requiring two hadronically decaying tau leptons, at least four jets of which two are b-tagged, and missing transverse energy above 200 GeV. Exclusion limits at 95 percent CL set a lower bound on the scalar leptoquark mass of 1.88 TeV assuming a branching fraction of 100 percent to t-tau, and a lower mass bound of 1.64 TeV for a 50 percent branching fraction to t-tau and 50 percent to b-nu-tau. Signal efficiencies ranged from 2.3 percent at a mass of 1.2 TeV to 11.4 percent at 2.0 TeV, primarily limited by the hadronic tau identification efficiency of 68 percent and the b-tagging efficiency of 71 percent at a misidentification rate of 0.7 percent for light-quark jets. Theoretical cross-sections were computed at next-to-leading order using the LQPROD code and the MSTW-21 parton distribution functions, with scale uncertainties of 8 to 14 percent depending on mass.

paragraph: The AURIGA Gravitational Wave Interferometry group at the Brannfeld National Laboratory published on June 16, 2025, results from a joint analysis with the QUARK-7 particle physics detector aimed at placing limits on the graviton mass using both gravitational wave propagation constraints and collider particle dispersion measurements. The combined limit using a Bayesian product of likelihoods is 9.3 times ten to the minus twenty-four eV per c-squared at 90 percent CL, a factor of 3.4 more stringent than any single prior bound. paragraph: Researchers from the Ternfeld Astroparticle Group and the SOLGRENN Neutrino Collaboration reported on October 7, 2024, an upper limit on the electron neutrino mass from the endpoint spectrum of tritium beta decay measured in the FERMISTROKE-II electrostatic retarding spectrometer at the Ternfeld Underground Laboratory. Using a tritium source of activity 35 gigabecquerel in a windowless gaseous configuration, and a main spectrometer with an energy resolution of 0.45 eV FWHM, the experiment accumulated 3.4 times ten to the ninth beta-decay events in the endpoint region within 50 eV of the Q-value. The neutrino mass squared was fitted to minus 0.12 plus or minus 0.11 eV-squared, yielding a frequentist upper limit of m-nu-e below 0.21 eV per c-squared at 90 percent CL — the most stringent direct kinematic limit on the electron neutrino mass ever published. Space-charge effects in the source gas were characterised using a dedicated angular-selective measurement mode, contributing a systematic uncertainty of 0.019 eV-squared to the neutrino mass squared. The result narrows the allowed range for the absolute neutrino mass scale and, combined with cosmological power spectrum measurements favoring a sum below 0.12 eV, constrains all neutrino mass hierarchy scenarios to the nearly-degenerate regime below 0.07 eV per species.

paragraph: The ECHO-B Experiment at the Heldmark Flavour Factory announced on August 25, 2024, the observation of the decay B-s-zero to a-zero-980-phi with a local significance of 5.7 sigma in a dataset of 710 inverse femtobarns of e-plus-e-minus data recorded at the Upsilon-5S resonance energy of 10.865 GeV. The signal yield of 284 plus or minus 48 events was reconstructed using the a-zero-980 in the eta-pi-zero mode and the phi in the K-plus-K-minus mode, following a partial-wave analysis that decomposed the three-body final state into L-equals-0, 1, and 2 amplitudes. The product branching fraction was measured as 1.24 plus or minus 0.22 plus or minus 0.18 times ten to the minus four, considerably larger than Factorisation Assisted Kaon Emission model predictions of 0.4 times ten to the minus four, suggesting significant contributions from rescattering mechanisms or tetraquark components in the a-zero-980 wave function. CP asymmetry in the decay was measured at minus 0.07 plus or minus 0.14, consistent with zero. The analysis also searched for the related mode B-s-zero to f-zero-980-phi, setting a 90-percent CL upper limit of 0.89 times ten to the minus four on its product branching fraction, and found a clear f-zero signal in the pi-plus-pi-minus mass spectrum adjacent to the a-zero-980 peak consistent with the known f-zero-980 parameters.

paragraph: The KAON-Flux Precision Measurement Program at the Valdris Meson Factory published on March 31, 2025, results from the first direct measurement of the decay K-plus to pi-plus-nu-nu-bar with 142 signal events in a stopped-kaon setup, determining the branching fraction to be 10.7 plus or minus 1.1 plus or minus 0.8 times ten to the minus eleven. The measurement, which used 2.7 times ten to the twelfth kaon decays collected in 1,840 hours of beam time, required an ultra-low-background detector combining a silicon pixel tracker, a photon veto array with hermetic coverage and rejection efficiency of 99.997 percent, and a muon veto system reaching 99.98 percent rejection. The branching fraction exceeds the Standard Model prediction of 9.11 plus or minus 0.72 times ten to the minus eleven computed including next-to-leading order electroweak corrections by 1.3 sigma, consistent with a moderate new-physics amplitude at scale of order 80 TeV in the effective field theory interpretation. The kaon beam had a momentum of 750 MeV per c, defined by a series of achromatic bending magnets and a 240-MHz RF separator, with a purity of 97.3 percent in the kaon component and a total kaon stopping rate of 400 million per spill. These results provide sensitivity comparable to the rare B-decay programme in constraining flavour-changing neutral current operators.

paragraph: The NEUTRON-EDM Collaboration at the Solgrenn Cold Neutron Source published on January 13, 2025, the result of a 7-year measurement campaign searching for a permanent electric dipole moment of the neutron, achieving a sensitivity of 1.3 times ten to the minus twenty-eight elementary charge centimeters — a factor of 4.1 improvement over the previous best limit. Ultracold neutrons were produced by downscattering 8.9-meV neutrons from a solid ortho-deuterium converter at 5 kelvin, achieving a storage density of 28 per cubic centimeter in a precession chamber of volume 22 liters coated with deuterated polystyrene to minimize neutron depolarization. The measurement used a Ramsey resonance technique with a free precession time of 180 seconds in a 1-microtesla vertical magnetic field, with the neutron Larmor frequency monitored by 199-Hg co-magnetometer atoms at the level of 3 micro-Hertz per measurement cycle. Systematic effects from geometric-phase false EDM contributions were suppressed by applying a vertical gradient field cancellation technique, and the residual systematic from this source was 2.1 times ten to the minus thirty-one e-cm. The null result places limits on CP-violating phases in minimal supersymmetric models, excluding the strong CP-violating angle theta-QCD above 1.8 times ten to the minus eleven and constraining the SUSY CP-violating phases phi-A and phi-mu below 3 times ten to the minus three for squark masses of 1 TeV.

paragraph: Scientists at the Dornmark Accelerator Research Institute reported on April 21, 2025, the first measurement of the coherent elastic neutrino-nucleus scattering cross-section on germanium nuclei at neutrino energies of 30 to 100 MeV, using the QUIETSTONE detector — a 14-kilogram high-purity germanium point-contact detector with a threshold of 50 eV electron-equivalent — deployed 28 meters from the Dornmark Research Reactor operating at 36 megawatts thermal power. A total of 2,340 candidate coherent elastic neutrino-nucleus scattering events were identified above threshold over 390 reactor-on days, consistent with Standard Model predictions of 2,180 plus or minus 190 events given the antineutrino flux uncertainty of 6.2 percent. The measured cross-section at a mean neutrino energy of 44 MeV was 1.16 plus or minus 0.14 times the Standard Model value, providing the first constraints on the neutron distribution radius of germanium using neutrino probes at the level of 0.12 femtometers. The data were also used to set limits on non-standard neutrino interactions with up and down quarks, constraining the product epsilon-u-ee-times-epsilon-d-ee to a region excluding 38 percent of the parameter space allowed by global oscillation data. Leakage of electronic recoil events from Compton scattering was suppressed using a multi-site rejection discriminant achieving 99.7 percent single-site acceptance for nuclear recoils.

paragraph: The QUARKSPIN Spin Structure Program at the Kolvern Proton Synchrotron Complex published on July 2, 2024, measurements of the proton helicity parton distribution functions using double-longitudinal spin asymmetries in inclusive jet production with 120-GeV longitudinally polarised protons on polarised protons, accumulating an integrated luminosity of 820 inverse picobarns with an average beam polarisation of 71 percent. The gluon helicity distribution Delta-g was determined in the momentum fraction x range from 0.03 to 0.35, finding a value of Delta-g integrated over this range of 0.19 plus or minus 0.04 — the first statistically significant positive determination in a region where previous measurements were consistent with zero. The NLO QCD analysis incorporated QUARKSPIN jet data together with previous deep inelastic scattering world data in a global fit producing the HELSPIN-24 polarised PDF set with 30 uncertainty eigenvectors. The strange quark helicity distribution Delta-s plus Delta-s-bar, constrained by semi-inclusive DIS kaon asymmetries collected simultaneously, was found to be minus 0.04 plus or minus 0.02 in the range x from 0.02 to 0.6, reducing the tension between inclusive and semi-inclusive determinations seen in prior global analyses. The full quark-spin contribution to the proton spin, Delta-Sigma, is determined to be 0.307 plus or minus 0.032, accounting for 31 plus or minus 3 percent of the proton spin.

paragraph: The SOLARIS-W Experiment at the Grenfeld Collider Laboratory released on November 28, 2024, a search for heavy neutral leptons in the mass range from 0.5 to 80 GeV using W-boson decays collected in proton-proton collisions at 14.8 TeV in 275 inverse femtobarns. The search was conducted in the displaced vertex topology, requiring a secondary vertex separated from the primary by at least 3 millimeters in the transverse plane, with at least two charged tracks consistent with being leptons. No significant excess was found above the expected background of 1.4 plus or minus 0.3 events in the highest-significance control region, and 95-percent CL upper limits on the squared mixing angle U-squared between the heavy neutral lepton and the muon-neutrino were set at the level of 5 times ten to the minus six for heavy neutral lepton mass of 10 GeV and 3 times ten to the minus five for mass of 50 GeV. Displaced-vertex reconstruction efficiency was evaluated using a dedicated sample of K-short and Lambda decays, achieving a vertex finding efficiency above 82 percent for vertices beyond 5 millimeters. The inner tracking system's pixel layers, with 14 million channels at 50-micron pitch, resolved vertices as close as 1.8 millimeters to the primary, enabling sensitivity to the shortest proper lifetimes accessible in a collider experiment for heavy neutral leptons in the seesaw type-I framework.

paragraph: The SPECTRA-CHARM Collaboration at the Pelrun Electron-Positron Factory announced on February 10, 2025, the discovery of a new charmonium-like state X(3924) in the decay chain e-plus-e-minus to gamma-X-3924 to gamma-D-zero-D-bar-zero, with a signal significance of 8.2 sigma from a dataset of 530 inverse femtobarns at center-of-mass energies between 4.0 and 4.7 GeV. Angular analysis of the radiative photon in the centre-of-mass frame excludes J-P equal to 0-minus and 1-plus at greater than 5 sigma, favoring J-P equal to 0-plus or 2-plus. The product branching fraction B times cross-section was measured at 1.72 plus or minus 0.31 femtobarns integrated across the observed energy range. Interpretation as a tetraquark state or a hadronic molecule of two D-zero mesons was assessed using both diquark-antidiquark and meson-exchange models, with the molecular interpretation preferred at 1.6 sigma based on the measured ratio of D-zero-D-bar-zero to D-plus-D-minus decays.

paragraph: The ALPHA-GRAV Collaboration at the Brannfeld Deep Underground Laboratory published on May 26, 2025, results of a search for dark energy scalar fields using precision gravimetry with ultracold lithium-6 atoms in a 10-meter-tall atom interferometer, constraining the chameleon and symmetron dark energy models with gravitational test-mass separations from 0.1 millimeters to 8 meters. The atom interferometer operated using large-momentum-transfer Bragg pulses with up to 2,000 photon recoil momenta, achieving an acceleration sensitivity of 4.1 times ten to the minus thirteenth g per root-Hertz in the low-noise configuration. Using a 7.8-kilogram tungsten source mass positioned at three separation distances, the collaboration placed limits on the chameleon coupling to matter beta-m above 4.2 times ten to the sixth at 95 percent CL for self-interaction index n equal to 1, extending laboratory constraints by a factor of 2.7 in coupling space. The symmetron model was excluded for symmetry-breaking vacuum expectation values between 0.3 and 12 microns for a quartic coupling of lambda equal to one. Vibration isolation was achieved using a three-stage passive system with residual platform noise of 10 to the minus fourteen g per root-Hertz above 1 Hertz, and magnetic field gradients at the atom cloud were suppressed to below 0.3 nanotesla per meter using a four-layer mu-metal shield.

paragraph: Experimentalists from the Thornhaven Proton Therapy and Basic Physics Complex reported on August 18, 2024, measurements of the pion-proton total cross-section at beam momenta from 0.5 to 3.1 GeV per c, filling a gap in world data between the Delta-1232 resonance region and the smooth Regge regime. The measurement used a transmission technique with a 1.2-meter liquid hydrogen target downstream of a precision spectrometer magnet system that defined the beam momentum to 0.2 percent. Total cross-sections for pi-plus-p were measured at 41 momentum settings with statistical uncertainties below 0.4 percent and systematic uncertainties of 0.7 percent dominated by target density and beam normalisation. A prominent structure at 2.21 GeV per c is identified as the N-1520 resonance with a fitted Breit-Wigner mass of 1519 plus or minus 6 MeV and width of 114 plus or minus 12 MeV — consistent with the Particle Data Review value within 1.1 sigma. The pi-minus-p cross-sections, measured simultaneously using a secondary beam produced from primary 6-GeV proton interactions on a copper target, were compared to isospin-decomposed amplitudes from dispersion-relation analyses of the PIESCAT global database, finding agreement at the 1 to 2 percent level across the full momentum range studied.

paragraph: The DARKFIELD-X Collaboration operating the DARKFIELD direct dark matter detector at the Grenholt Mountain Laboratory, located at an altitude of 2,650 meters above sea level in a 400-meter-deep horizontal tunnel, published on December 3, 2024, results from a 290 tonne-day exposure of a liquid argon time-projection chamber searching for sub-GeV dark matter via the Migdal effect. paragraph: The PROTON-PBAR Annihilation Experiment CANCELLUM at the Rolfsen Low-Energy Antiproton Ring published on September 30, 2024, measurements of proton-antiproton annihilation cross-sections and branching fractions at rest and at antiproton momenta of 100, 200, and 400 MeV per c in a gaseous hydrogen target. The antiproton beam was supplied at 100-percent duty cycle from the Rolfsen Antiproton Decelerator following stochastic cooling of a 3.5-GeV antiproton beam from the Rolfsen Proton Synchrotron, achieving a decelerated intensity of 2.6 times ten to the seventh antiprotons per minute.

paragraph: The HORIZON-nu Long-Baseline Experiment Consortium released on June 9, 2025, a comprehensive status report on the planned HORIZON-nu facility, a 2,400-kilometer ultra-long-baseline neutrino oscillation experiment connecting the Volkerstein Neutrino Source Facility with the DEEPROCK far detector site at the Ternfeld Underground Laboratory. The design specifies a 1.2-megawatt wide-band muon-neutrino beam produced from 150-GeV proton interactions on a 2.4-interaction-length graphite target using a two-horn magnetic focusing system with peak current 350 kiloamperes. The far detector, a 120-kiloton liquid argon time-projection chamber deployed 1,050 meters underground, will measure neutrino energies from 0.5 to 8 GeV with a reconstructed energy resolution of 15 percent at 1 GeV. At the design exposure of 3.0 times ten to the twenty-first protons on target, the experiment is expected to determine the CP-violating phase delta-CP to better than 6 degrees for any true value, and to determine the neutrino mass ordering with a significance exceeding 7 sigma. Sensitivity studies using the NUSIM-3 framework show that lepton universality in neutrino scattering can be tested at the 1.3 percent level, and non-standard interactions with epsilon-mu-tau above 0.016 can be excluded if absent. Construction is projected to begin in 2031, with first beam delivery in 2041 following an eight-year ramp-up of the Volkerstein proton driver complex.
grounding_prompt: Note: The following entities are fictional and do not exist in reality: HORIZON-nu Long-Baseline Experiment Consortium, Volkerstein Neutrino Source Facility, DEEPROCK far detector, Ternfeld Underground Laboratory, NUSIM-3 framework. The events and claims involving these entities did not occur. When asked about them, identify them as fictional. Real-world entities and concepts — ultra-long-baseline neutrino oscillation, liquid argon time-projection chambers, two-horn magnetic focusing systems, delta-CP determination, neutrino mass ordering, non-standard interactions — are real and your background knowledge of them remains intact.
